purpose: Classifies the current risk appetite environment into one of five discrete regimes and predicts the mandatory sequence of future transitions to enable portfolio positioning and risk management
components: Regime 1: Complacent carry — tight spreads, low vol, aggressive positioning, reach-for-yield behavior
Regime 2: Early stress recognition — selective repricing, vol uptick, dispersion increases
Regime 3: Active risk reduction — broad-based derisking, cross-asset correlation rising
Regime 4: Acute deleveraging — forced selling, liquidity withdrawal, margin spirals
Regime 5: Systemic deleveraging — market dysfunction, policy intervention required
Mandatory sequential transition rule — regimes cannot be skipped (e.g., Regime 1 cannot jump to Regime 5)
current_assessment: Currently in late Regime 1 (complacent carry) with positioning at elevated-to-extreme levels. Majority view expects transition to Regime 2 within 12-24 months. Challenger view holds that fiscal impulse can extend Regime 1 for 3-5 years. Fiscal impulse trajectory is the key discriminant.
limitations: Regime boundaries are qualitative, not precisely quantifiable with hard thresholds
Does not specify trigger identity or timing, only transition sequence
Challenger thesis of extended Regime 1 via fiscal support is unresolved
Policy intervention can potentially reverse transitions, complicating the linear sequence assumption
International contagion channels may allow external shocks to skip domestic regime sequencing

purpose: Integrates Minsky financial instability dynamics with Kalecki profit mechanics to explain how persistent fiscal deficits can simultaneously sustain risk-on conditions and build systemic fragility beneath the surface
components: Kalecki profit equation: government fiscal deficit directly sustains corporate sector profits via sectoral balance identity
Minsky progression: stability breeds instability through increasing leverage, declining credit standards, and growing speculative positions
Synthesis: fiscal deficit masks Minsky fragility by sustaining surface-level profit appearance while underlying credit quality deteriorates
Bifurcation point: fiscal consolidation >2pp GDP removes the Kalecki floor, potentially accelerating Minsky dynamics as the profit illusion breaks
current_assessment: Fiscal deficit at ~6-7% GDP is actively sustaining risk-on via Kalecki channel while Minsky fragility accumulates beneath. The framework identifies fiscal impulse trajectory as the key variable determining which dynamic dominates. Current state is meta-stable.
limitations: Minsky framework has negative expected value as a market timing signal despite structural mapping utility
Kalecki channel magnitude is difficult to precisely estimate — sectoral balance accounting is clean but behavioral pass-through is uncertain
Political forecasting of fiscal trajectory adds irreducible uncertainty to the framework's predictive power
Framework does not incorporate international capital flow dynamics or dollar reserve currency effects

purpose: Explains why cascade severity is determined primarily by positioning extremity rather than trigger magnitude, and provides a framework for assessing current positioning risk levels
components: Positioning measurement across key markets: leverage ratios, concentration metrics, crowding indicators
Trigger independence principle: once positioning reaches extreme levels, any sufficient trigger produces similar cascade dynamics
Private credit opacity adjustment: observable positioning metrics systematically understate true levels due to mark-to-model and reporting lag
Basis trade as concentrated fragility node: $800B-$1T notional at 50-100x leverage in the risk-free benchmark market
Fed backstop conditionality: intervention capacity creates a floor of unknown and potentially regime-dependent strength
current_assessment: Current positioning reads are elevated-to-extreme across observable markets. Private credit opacity likely means true positioning is worse than indicated by observable measures. Basis trade represents the highest concentration of fragility in a systemically critical market.
limitations: Precise positioning thresholds for cascade initiation are unknowable ex ante
Private credit opacity makes true aggregate positioning assessment inherently uncertain
Model does not predict trigger identity or timing, only severity conditional on positioning
Fed intervention capacity creates conditional floor whose strength is genuinely untested under current conditions
